Note that aged is pronounced /eɪdʒd/    when it means 'years old’  (as in He has a rugged /'rʌgid/                        one/three/four-legged /'legid/ ragged /'rægɪd/                           wretched /'retʃid/ naked /'neɪkɪd/                         wicked /'wɪkɪd/ cursed /'kɜ:sid/                         sacred /'seɪkrɪd/ crooked /'krokɪd/                      learned /'lɜ:nɪd/ beloved /bɪ'lʌvid/                     dogged /'dngɪd/ aged /'eɪdʒɪd/    (= very old)        blessed /'blesɪd/ pronounced /ɪd/   instead of /d/  or /t/ (►  44.2).

A  few adjectives ending in -ed have a special pronunciation: the last syllable is 191  pronunciation          of  aged,      naked,       etc not What's the length of the runway? (unmarked) (     What's the shortness...?) - The worst thing about the film was its length. (marked) not What is her exact age? (unmarked) (     What is her exact youth?) - Age brings wisdom but I'd rather have youth and stupidity. (marked) Some nouns are used in similar 'unmarked’  ways. Compare: not Scales measure how heavy things are. (unmarked) (     ... how light...) - Lead is one of the heaviest metals. (marked) not He's only twenty-three years old. (unmarked) (     .. .years young.) - Will you still love me when I'm old? (marked) Section15 Adjectives grammar •  Section 16   Adverbs and Adverbials adjectives. This can cause problems for some learners.

Note that not all languages make a grammatical distinction between adverbs and (e.g.  When I was a student), ►    Sections 20, 22-23.

Section they are called 'adverbials* when necessary. For adverbial clauses whether they are adverbs, or longer expressions functioning like adverbs. In this It can be convenient to use a single word to talk about modifiers of this kind, When I was a student, I went to the gym every day.

She went all the way up the stairs.

Please answer right away.

You did that quite remarkably well.

way as adverbs. To  be honest, I think she's crazy.

Other longer expressions can modify sentences, clauses, verbs, etc in the same When I was a student, I went to the gym regularly.

She went right up the stairs.

Please answer now.

You did that very well.

Frankly, I think she's crazy.

(but not nouns).

modify (add meaning to) sentences, clauses or various parts of clauses for example frankly, now, very, right, regularly.
